hypothesis: B13's aggregate flops_used (27.49e9) was already close to the champion's (27.35e9), while effective_compute (34.80e9) was ~7.3e9 higher -- consistent with a largely fixed per-call overhead from the 128 power-iteration + 32 diagonal calls, independent of main-sampling row count. The 16-node Gauss-Hermite quadrature gives zero sampling variance along the dominant direction, so this estimator should be more sample-efficient per FLOP than plain MC. Halving the main pair-count scale factor (3250->1625) should roughly halve the dominant raw-FLOP term while the fixed overhead stays constant, netting a real effective_compute reduction despite some MSE increase from fewer orthogonal-complement draws.

evaluation.command: uv run --frozen whest run --estimator <estimator> --runner subprocess --dataset hf://aicrowd/arc-whestbench-public-2026@v1-phase1 --split mini --flop-budget 272000000000 --format json

reports.note: Champion run fresh (not reused from an earlier experiment) this time, back-to-back with the candidate, since effective_compute has real wall-clock-derived measurement noise and this experiment's outcome hinges on a delicate compute-vs-accuracy tradeoff near the promotion threshold.

aggregate.note: Candidate's multiplier hit exactly 0.1 -- it overshot below the 2.72e10 floor (effective_compute=18.31e9), meaning it paid MSE cost for sample reduction beyond the point of any further multiplier benefit. The floor boundary leaves 2.72e10-1.83e10=8.89e9 of 'free' compute headroom unused.

calibration_for_followup.purpose: Two data points (this run's candidate, and B13's aggregate numbers) let a linear effective_compute-vs-pair-scale model be fit to find the pair-scale that lands effective_compute right at the 2.72e10 floor boundary -- the point of zero additional multiplier benefit from cutting further, avoiding B15's overshoot.
calibration_for_followup.linear_fit: slope ~= (34.80e9-18.31e9)/(3250-1625) ~= 1.015e7 per unit pair_scale; solving 18.31e9 + slope*(X-1625) = 27.2e9 gives X ~= 2500
calibration_for_followup.recommendation: Retry with pair_scale ~2500 (instead of 3250 or 1625). This should land effective_compute close to the 2.72e10 floor (multiplier ~0.1, same as this run) but with ~54% more orthogonal-complement samples than this run, meaningfully reducing MSE back toward B13's level while keeping the multiplier at its floor-clamped minimum -- strictly better than both B13 (wasted overhead above the floor) and B15 (wasted floor headroom).

reason: Overshot the sample-count reduction: MSE increased 57% (8.505e-06 -> 1.3376e-05) while the multiplier only dropped ~10% (0.1107 -> 0.1, floor-clamped), so the net score is 42% worse than champion, not better. The core premise (fixed overhead is a real, exploitable inefficiency) is still supported by the flops_used/effective_compute gap, but halving was too large a cut -- it pushed effective_compute 8.89e9 below the floor with no further multiplier benefit, purely losing accuracy for nothing past that boundary. A precisely-calibrated smaller cut (pair_scale ~2500, derived from this run's and B13's data) should avoid both failure modes.
